title: Bacterial Vaginosis - STI Treatment Guidelines
text:
2% vaginal cream single dose versus clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime for 7 days among 540 women with BV, no statistically significant difference existed between groups in clinical cure at days 21–30 (64.3% versus 63.2%; p = 0.95) ( 1019 ); however, this study had methodologic problems. A patient savings card for Clindesse 2% vaginal cream is available at https://www.clindesse.com/pdf/CLINDESSE_SavingsCard.pdf .
BV biofilm disrupting agents (i.e., TOL-463) ( 1020 ) are being investigated to determine their role in enhancing the likelihood of BV cure relative to approved therapies. Studies have evaluated the clinical and microbiologic efficacy of intravaginal Lactobacillus and other probiotic formulations to treat BV and restore normal vaginal microbiota ( 1021 – 1025 ); overall, no studies support these products as an adjunctive or replacement therapy for women with BV.
Other Management Considerations 
All women with BV should be tested for HIV and other STIs.
Follow-Up 
Follow-up visits are unnecessary if symptoms resolve. Because persistent or recurrent BV is common, women should be advised to return for evaluation if symptoms recur. Limited data are available regarding optimal management strategies for women with persistent or recurrent BV.
